modules.name: glue.pidfile
documentation:
A simple module for acquiring pidfile locks (e.g., for use by daemons).

Copyright (C) 2010 by Peter F. Couvares, Syracuse University
mailto: pfcouvar@syr.edu

functions.name: glue.pidfile.get_lock
documentation:
Tries to write a lockfile containing the current pid.  Excepts if
the lockfile already contains the pid of a running process.

Although this should prevent a lock from being granted twice, it
can theoretically deny a lock unjustly in the unlikely event that
the original process is gone but another unrelated process has
been assigned the same pid by the OS.

functions.name: glue.pidfile.confirm_lock
documentation:
Confirm that the given lockfile contains our pid.
Should be entirely unecessary, but paranoia always served me well.

From http://stackoverflow.com/questions/566746

functions.name: glue.text_progress_bar.demo
documentation: Demonstrate progress bar.

classes.name: glue.text_progress_bar.ProgressBar

methods.name: glue.text_progress_bar.ProgressBar.iterate
documentation:
Use as a target of a for-loop to issue a progress update for every
iteration. For example:

progress = ProgressBar()
for text in progress.iterate(["foo", "bar", "bat"]):
    ...

methods.name: glue.text_progress_bar.ProgressBar.show
documentation: Redraw the text progress bar.

methods.name: glue.text_progress_bar.ProgressBar.update
documentation:
Redraw the progress bar, optionally changing the value and text
and return the (possibly new) value.  For I/O performance, the
progress bar might not be written to the terminal if the text does
not change and the value changes by too little.  Use .show() to
force a redraw.

methods.name: glue.text_progress_bar.ProgressBar.increment
documentation:
Redraw the progress bar, incrementing the value by delta
(default=1) and optionally changing the text.  Returns the
ProgressBar's new value.  See also .update().

Display a text progress bar.

A final line feed is printed when the ProgressBar is garbage collected.
Explicitly deleting the object can force a line feed when desired.  As
an alternative, using the ProgressBar as a context manager will ensure
a final line feed is printed when the code block within which the
ProgressBar is being used exits.

Example:

```pycon
>>> with ProgressBar(max=3) as pb:
...    pb.update(1)
...    pb.update(2)
...    pb.update(3)
...
```

classes.name: glue.lal.LIGOTimeGPS

methods.name: glue.lal.LIGOTimeGPS.__init__
documentation:
Create a LIGOTimeGPS instance.  The first parameter is the
count of seconds, and the second (optional) parameter is the
count of nanoseconds.  If the nanoseconds parameter is not
supplied, it is assumed to be 0.  Either parameter can be
a numeric type or an ASCII string.

Example:

```pycon
>>> LIGOTimeGPS(100.5)
LIGOTimeGPS(100, 500000000)
>>> LIGOTimeGPS("100.5")
LIGOTimeGPS(100, 500000000)
>>> LIGOTimeGPS(100, 500000000)
LIGOTimeGPS(100, 500000000)
>>> LIGOTimeGPS(0, 100500000000)
LIGOTimeGPS(100, 500000000)
>>> LIGOTimeGPS(100.2, 300000000)
LIGOTimeGPS(100, 500000000)
>>> LIGOTimeGPS("0.000000001")
LIGOTimeGPS(0, 1)
>>> LIGOTimeGPS("0.0000000012")
LIGOTimeGPS(0, 1)
>>> LIGOTimeGPS("0.0000000018")
LIGOTimeGPS(0, 2)
>>> LIGOTimeGPS("-0.8")
LIGOTimeGPS(-1, 200000000)
>>> LIGOTimeGPS("-1.2")
LIGOTimeGPS(-2, 800000000)
```
basic class methods

methods.name: glue.lal.LIGOTimeGPS.__str__
documentation: Return an ASCII string representation of a LIGOTimeGPS.

methods.name: glue.lal.LIGOTimeGPS.__float__
documentation:
Convert a LIGOTimeGPS to seconds as a float.

Example:

```pycon
>>> float(LIGOTimeGPS(100.5))
100.5
```
type conversion

methods.name: glue.lal.LIGOTimeGPS.__int__
documentation:
Return the integer part (seconds) of a LIGOTimeGPS as an int.

Example:

```pycon
>>> int(LIGOTimeGPS(100.5))
100
```

methods.name: glue.lal.LIGOTimeGPS.__long__
documentation:
Return the integer part (seconds) of a LIGOTimeGPS as a long.

Example:

```pycon
>>> repr(long(LIGOTimeGPS(100.5))) == '100L' if sys.version_info.major < 3 else True
True
```

methods.name: glue.lal.LIGOTimeGPS.ns
documentation:
Convert a LIGOTimeGPS to a count of nanoseconds as a long.

Example:

```pycon
>>> LIGOTimeGPS(100.5).ns()
100500000000
```

methods.name: glue.lal.LIGOTimeGPS.__hash__
documentation: comparison

methods.name: glue.lal.LIGOTimeGPS.__lt__
documentation:
Compare a value to a LIGOTimeGPS.  If the value being compared
to the LIGOTimeGPS is not also a LIGOTimeGPS, then an attempt
is made to convert it to a LIGOTimeGPS.

Example:

```pycon
>>> LIGOTimeGPS(100.5) < LIGOTimeGPS(200)
True
>>> LIGOTimeGPS(100.5) < 200
True
>>> LIGOTimeGPS(100.5) < "200"
True
```

methods.name: glue.lal.LIGOTimeGPS.__bool__
documentation:
Return True if the LIGOTimeGPS is nonzero.

Example:

```pycon
>>> bool(LIGOTimeGPS(100.5))
True
```

methods.name: glue.lal.LIGOTimeGPS.__add__
documentation:
Add a value to a LIGOTimeGPS.  If the value being added to the
LIGOTimeGPS is not also a LIGOTimeGPS, then an attempt is made
to convert it to a LIGOTimeGPS.

Example:

```pycon
>>> LIGOTimeGPS(100.5) + LIGOTimeGPS(3)
LIGOTimeGPS(103, 500000000)
>>> LIGOTimeGPS(100.5) + 3
LIGOTimeGPS(103, 500000000)
>>> LIGOTimeGPS(100.5) + "3"
LIGOTimeGPS(103, 500000000)
```
arithmetic

methods.name: glue.lal.LIGOTimeGPS.__sub__
documentation:
Subtract a value from a LIGOTimeGPS.  If the value being
subtracted from the LIGOTimeGPS is not also a LIGOTimeGPS, then
an attempt is made to convert it to a LIGOTimeGPS.

Example:

```pycon
>>> LIGOTimeGPS(100.5) - LIGOTimeGPS(3)
LIGOTimeGPS(97, 500000000)
>>> LIGOTimeGPS(100.5) - 3
LIGOTimeGPS(97, 500000000)
>>> LIGOTimeGPS(100.5) - "3"
LIGOTimeGPS(97, 500000000)
```

methods.name: glue.lal.LIGOTimeGPS.__rsub__
documentation: Subtract a LIGOTimeGPS from a value.

methods.name: glue.lal.LIGOTimeGPS.__mul__
documentation:
Multiply a LIGOTimeGPS by a number.

Example:

```pycon
>>> LIGOTimeGPS(100.5) * 2
LIGOTimeGPS(201, 0)
```

methods.name: glue.lal.LIGOTimeGPS.__truediv__
documentation:
Divide a LIGOTimeGPS by a number.

Example:

```pycon
>>> LIGOTimeGPS(100.5) / 2
LIGOTimeGPS(50, 250000000)
```

methods.name: glue.lal.LIGOTimeGPS.__floordiv__
documentation: Integer division (not implemented).

methods.name: glue.lal.LIGOTimeGPS.__mod__
documentation:
Compute the remainder when a LIGOTimeGPS is divided by a number.

Example:

```pycon
>>> LIGOTimeGPS(100.5) % 3
LIGOTimeGPS(1, 500000000)
```

methods.name: glue.lal.LIGOTimeGPS.__pos__
documentation: unary arithmetic

An object for storing times with nanosecond resolution.  LAL defines an
equivalent object which is used through-out the search algorithms to
represent times.  Many LALApps routines input and output times in a
manner that meshes well with this object.

Internally the time is represented as a signed integer "seconds" part
and an unsigned integer "nanoseconds" part.  The actual time is always
constructed by adding the nanoseconds to the seconds.  So -0.5 s is
represented by setting seconds = -1, and nanoseconds to 500000000.
That's the way LAL does it.
High precision time object
Python version in case LAL isn't available

classes.name: glue.lal.CacheEntry
documentation: Define a class that will fail if invoked

classes.name: glue.lal.Cache

methods.name: glue.lal.Cache.fromfile
documentation: Return a Cache object whose entries are read from an open file.
methods to create new Cache objects

methods.name: glue.lal.Cache.fromfilenames
documentation:
Read Cache objects from the files named and concatenate the results into a
single Cache.

methods.name: glue.lal.Cache.from_urls
documentation:
Return a Cache whose entries are inferred from the URLs
in urllist, if possible.  PFN lists will also work; for PFNs, the path
will be absolutized and "file://" and "localhost" will be assumed
for the schemes and hosts.

The filenames must be in the format set forth by DASWG in T050017-00.

methods.name: glue.lal.Cache.__isub__
documentation: Remove elements from self that are in other.
some set arithmetic to make life better

methods.name: glue.lal.Cache.__sub__
documentation: Return a Cache containing the entries of self that are not in other.

methods.name: glue.lal.Cache.__ior__
documentation: Append entries from other onto self without introducing (new) duplicates.

methods.name: glue.lal.Cache.__or__
documentation: Return a Cache containing all entries of self and other.

methods.name: glue.lal.Cache.__iand__
documentation: Remove elements in self that are not in other.

methods.name: glue.lal.Cache.__and__
documentation: Return a Cache containing the entries of self that are also in other.

methods.name: glue.lal.Cache.unique
documentation:
Return a Cache which has every element of self, but without
duplication.  Preserve order.  Does not hash, so a bit slow.

methods.name: glue.lal.Cache.tofile
documentation: write a cache object to the fileobj as a lal cache file
other useful manipulations

methods.name: glue.lal.Cache.topfnfile
documentation: write a cache object to filename as a plain text pfn file

methods.name: glue.lal.Cache.to_segmentlistdict
documentation:
Return a segmentlistdict object describing the instruments
and times spanned by the entries in this Cache.  The return
value is coalesced.

methods.name: glue.lal.Cache.sieve
documentation:
Return a Cache object with those CacheEntries that
contain the given patterns (or overlap, in the case of
segment or segmentlist).  If exact_match is True, then
non-None ifos, description, and segment patterns must
match exactly, and a non-None segmentlist must contain
a segment which matches exactly).

It makes little sense to specify both segment and
segmentlist arguments, but it is not prohibited.

Bash-style wildcards (*?) are allowed for ifos and description.

methods.name: glue.lal.Cache.pfnlist
documentation: Return a list of physical file names.

methods.name: glue.lal.Cache.checkfilesexist
documentation:
Runs through the entries of the Cache() object and checks each entry
if the file which it points to exists or not. If the file does exist then
it adds the entry to the Cache() object containing found files, otherwise it
adds the entry to the Cache() object containing all entries that are missing.
It returns both in the follwing order: Cache_Found, Cache_Missed.

Pass on_missing to control how missing files are handled:
  "warn": print a warning message saying how many files
          are missing out of the total checked.
  "error": raise an exception if any are missing
  "ignore": do nothing

An object representing a LAL cache file. Currently it is possible to
add anything to a Cache. This method should check that the thing you
are adding is a CacheEntry and throw an error if it is not.
An object representing a LAL cache file

modules.name: glue.pipeline
documentation:
This modules contains objects that make it simple for the user to
create python scripts that build Condor DAGs to run code on the LSC
Data Grid.

methods.name: glue.pipeline.CondorJob.add_arg
glue.pipeline.CondorJob.add_file_arg
documentation:
Add a file argument to the executable. Arguments are appended after any
options and their order is guaranteed. Also adds the file name to the
list of required input data for this job.
@param filename: file to add as argument.

methods.name: glue.pipeline.CondorJob.get_args
documentation: Return the list of arguments that are to be passed to the executable.

methods.name: glue.pipeline.CondorJob.add_opt
documentation:
Add a command line option to the executable. The order that the arguments
will be appended to the command line is not guaranteed, but they will
always be added before any command line arguments. The name of the option
is prefixed with double hyphen and the program is expected to parse it
with getopt_long().
@param opt: command line option to add.
@param value: value to pass to the option (None for no argument).

methods.name: glue.pipeline.CondorJob.get_opt
glue.pipeline.CondorJob.add_file_opt
documentation:
Add a command line option to the executable. The order that the arguments
will be appended to the command line is not guaranteed, but they will
always be added before any command line arguments. The name of the option
is prefixed with double hyphen and the program is expected to parse it
with getopt_long().
@param opt: command line option to add.
@param value: value to pass to the option (None for no argument).

methods.name: glue.pipeline.CondorJob.get_opts
documentation: Return the dictionary of opts for the job.

methods.name: glue.pipeline.CondorJob.add_short_opt
documentation:
Add a command line option to the executable. The order that the arguments
will be appended to the command line is not guaranteed, but they will
always be added before any command line arguments. The name of the option
is prefixed with single hyphen and the program is expected to parse it
with getopt() or getopt_long() (if a single character option), or
getopt_long_only() (if multiple characters).  Long and (single-character)
short options may be mixed if the executable permits this.
@param opt: command line option to add.
@param value: value to pass to the option (None for no argument).

classes.name: glue.pipeline.CondorDAGJob

methods.name: glue.pipeline.CondorDAGJob.__init__
documentation:
universe = the condor universe to run the job in.
executable = the executable to run in the DAG.

methods.name: glue.pipeline.CondorDAGJob.create_node
documentation:
Create a condor node from this job. This provides a basic interface to
the CondorDAGNode class. Most jobs in a workflow will subclass the
CondorDAGNode class and overwrite this to give more details when
initializing the node. However, this will work fine for jobs with very simp
input/output.

methods.name: glue.pipeline.CondorDAGJob.add_var_opt
documentation:
Add a variable (or macro) option to the condor job. The option is added
to the submit file and a different argument to the option can be set for
each node in the DAG.
@param opt: name of option to add.

methods.name: glue.pipeline.CondorDAGJob.add_var_condor_cmd
documentation:
Add a condor command to the submit file that allows variable (macro)
arguments to be passes to the executable.

methods.name: glue.pipeline.CondorDAGJob.add_var_arg
documentation:
Add a command to the submit file to allow variable (macro) arguments
to be passed to the executable.

A Condor DAG job never notifies the user on completion and can have variable
options that are set for a particular node in the DAG. Inherits methods
from a CondorJob.

methods.name: glue.pipeline.CondorDAGNode.add_macro

Add a variable (macro) for this node.  This can be different for
each node in the DAG, even if they use the same CondorJob.  Within
the CondorJob, the value of the macro can be referenced as
'$(name)' -- for instance, to define a unique output or error file
for each node.
@param name: macro name.

the options for this instance of the node and not those

methods.name: glue.pipeline.CondorDAGNode.add_var_condor_cmd
documentation:
Add a variable (macro) condor command for this node. If the command
specified does not exist in the CondorJob, it is added so the submit file
will be correct.
PLEASE NOTE: AS with other add_var commands, the variable must be set for
all nodes that use the CondorJob instance.
@param command: command name
@param value: Value of the command for this node in the DAG.

Add a variable (or macro) argument to the condor job. The argument is

for each node in the DAG.

methods.name: glue.pipeline.CondorDAGNode.add_file_arg

Add a variable (or macro) file name argument to the condor job. The
argument is added to the submit file and a different value of the
argument can be set for each node in the DAG. The file name is also
added to the list of input files.
@param filename: name of option to add.

The finalize method of a node is called before the node is

A CondorDAGNode represents a node in the DAG. It corresponds to a particular
condor job (and so a particular submit file). If the job has variable
(macro) options, they can be set here so each nodes executes with the
correct options.

methods.name: glue.pipeline.CondorDAG.write_script
documentation:
Write the workflow to a script (.sh instead of .dag).

Assuming that parents were added to the DAG before their children,
dependencies should be handled correctly.

A CondorDAG is a Condor Directed Acyclic Graph that describes a collection
of Condor jobs and the order in which to run them. All Condor jobs in the
DAG must write their Codor logs to the same file.
NOTE: The log file must not be on an NFS mounted system as the Condor jobs
must be able to get an exclusive file lock on the log file.

Returns the name of the channel that this job is filtering. Note that

A ScienceSegment is a period of time where the experimenters determine
that the inteferometer is in a state where the data is suitable for
scientific analysis. A science segment can have a list of AnalysisChunks
asscociated with it that break the segment up into (possibly overlapping)
smaller time intervals for analysis.

documentation:
Parse the science segments from the segwizard output contained in file.
@param filename: input text file containing a list of science segments generated by
segwizard.
@param min_length: only append science segments that are longer than min_length.
@param slide_sec: Slide each ScienceSegment by:

```
delta > 0:
  [s, e] -> [s+delta, e].
delta < 0:
  [s, e] -> [s, e-delta].
```

@param buffer: shrink the ScienceSegment:

```
[s, e] -> [s+buffer, e-buffer]
```

contain multiple ScienceSegments and has a method to generate these from
a text file produces by the LIGOtools segwizard program.

methods.name: glue.pipeline.LsyncCache.group
documentation:
Group an iterable into an n-tuples iterable. Incomplete
tuples are discarded

methods.name: glue.pipeline.LsyncCache.parse
documentation:
Each line of the frame cache file is like the following:

/frames/E13/LHO/frames/hoftMon_H1/H-H1_DMT_C00_L2-9246,H,H1_DMT_C00_L2,1,16 1240664820 6231       {924600000 924646720 924646784 924647472 924647712 924700000}

The description is as follows:

1.1) Directory path of files
1.2) Site
1.3) Type
1.4) Number of frames in the files (assumed to be 1)
1.5) Duration of the frame files.

2) UNIX timestamp for directory modification time.

3) Number of files that that match the above pattern in the directory.

4) List of time range or segments [start, stop)

We store the cache for each site and frameType combination
as a dictionary where the keys are (directory, duration)
tuples and the values are segment lists.

Since the cache file is already coalesced we do not
have to call the coalesce method on the segment lists.

classes.name: glue.pipeline.LSCDataFindJob

methods.name: glue.pipeline.LSCDataFindJob.__init__
glue.pipeline.LSCDataFindJob.get_cache_dir
documentation: returns the directroy that the cache files are written to.

methods.name: glue.pipeline.LSCDataFindJob.get_config_file
documentation: return the configuration file object

An LSCdataFind job used to locate data. The static options are
read from the section [datafind] in the ini file. The stdout from
LSCdataFind contains the paths to the frame files and is directed to a file
in the cache directory named by site and GPS start and end times. The stderr
is directed to the logs directory. The job always runs in the scheduler
universe. The path to the executable is determined from the ini file.

A cbc sqlite job adds to CondorDAGJob and AnalysisJob features common to jobs
which read or write to a sqlite database. Of note, the universe is always set to
local regardless of what's in the cp file, the extension is set
to None so that it may be set by individual SqliteNodes, log files do not
have macrogpsstarttime and endtime in them, and get_env is set to True.

and set_database methods.

classes.name: glue.pipeline.DeepCopyableConfigParser
documentation:
The standard SafeConfigParser no longer supports deepcopy() as of python
2.7 (see http://bugs.python.org/issue16058). This subclass restores that
functionality.
